ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for 'peanut crisp' as a Chinese palace-style confection; closest parent is peanut cookie (FOODON_03301710).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Biscuits and cookies) used as closest parent; no specific code for Chinese peanut crisp confection.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Chinese peanut confection.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 6 pieces (approximately 60 g), as sold by the restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Peanut Cookies / Peanut Biscuits — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Cookies, peanut butter, commercially prepared, regular) and USDA Foundation Foods (Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese palace-style peanut crisp in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA. Values adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for peanut butter cookies (FDC ID 18166) and cross-referenced with USDA peanut dry-roasted data (FDC ID 172430) to reflect a peanut-dominant crisp with wheat flour and sugar. Energy and macros adjusted to reflect higher peanut content (~40% peanut by weight) and lower moisture typical of a crisp/shortbread-style confection. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of cookies and biscuits — analogous estimate based on shortbread/peanut cookie data from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Chinese palace-style peanut crisp. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous peanut butter cookie / shortbread entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 40–65 for peanut-based biscuits and cookies). A GI of 55 is considered the upper limit for a low GI food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 34.8) / 100 ≈ 19.2, where carbohydrate per 60 g serving = 58.0 × 0.60 = 34.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g; ~126 mg per 60 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces per occasion, as the moderate sodium content from added salt in the dough can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets when consumed in larger quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) and moderate glycemic load (~19 per 60 g serving) driven by refined wheat flour and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat peanut crisp as an occasional snack only; the low GI (at the upper limit of low) and significant sugar content (~13 g per 60 g serving) can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise, and portion control to 1–2 pieces is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 26 g per 100 g with saturated fat 4.5 g; peanut-derived unsaturated fats are cardioprotective but energy density is high
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the predominant fats in peanut crisp are heart-healthy mono- and polyunsaturated fats from peanuts, the high energy density and moderate saturated fat content mean that individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict intake to small portions and account for this snack within their overall daily fat and calorie budget.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut Crisp (花生酥, Huā shēng sū) is a traditional Chinese confection made primarily from ground or whole roasted peanuts combined with wheat flour, sugar, and oil, then shaped and baked or lightly fried to produce a crisp, crumbly texture. The version sold in Singapore by See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) is described as a palace-style handcrafted dessert served in a set of 6 pieces, noted for its lifelike sculptural appearance and rich peanut aroma. The flavour profile is sweet and savoury, with a pronounced roasted peanut fragrance and a delicate, melt-in-the-mouth crunch. Per 100 g, peanut crisp provides approximately 498 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 26 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanuts), and 58 g carbohydrate with 22 g sugar. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), yielding a glycemic load of roughly 19 per standard 60 g serving. Clinically, the low GI (at the upper limit of low) and sugar content require portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The moderate sodium level (~210 mg/100 g) is relevant for hypertension management. The peanut-derived unsaturated fat profile is broadly favourable for cardiovascular health, though the high caloric density warrants moderation. Contains wheat (gluten) and peanut allergens; not suitable for those with peanut allergy or coeliac disease. No preservatives are added according to the product description.
common_user_queries: How many calories in peanut crisp?
Is peanut crisp suitable for diabetics?
What allergens are in peanut crisp?
What is the GI of peanut crisp?
How much sugar is in peanut crisp?
What is a Chinese palace dessert?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chinese palace-style peanut crisp was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA SR Legacy peanut butter cookie data (FDC ID 18166) and USDA peanut dry-roasted data (FDC ID 172430), adjusted for the higher peanut content and lower moisture of a crisp-style confection. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable peanut-based biscuit entries. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (peanut butter cookies, FDC ID 18166) and USDA Foundation Foods (dry-roasted peanuts, FDC ID 172430); no exact match was available in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this specific Chinese palace-style peanut crisp. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as no trans fats are expected in a traditionally prepared peanut crisp without hydrogenated oils; confirmed zero by analogy with USDA source. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0 as this is a plant-based confection containing no animal-derived ingredients that would contribute B12; naturally zero. 4. vitamin_c_mg is set to 0.0 as baking destroys residual vitamin C in peanuts; confirmed zero by analogy with USDA baked peanut cookie data. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1.0 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy peanut cookie source entry. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1.0 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy peanut cookie source entry. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on peanut butter cookie / shortbread entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this specific product was found. A GI of 55 is considered low. 8. Serving size of 60 g (6 pieces) is based on the product description (6 Pcs) with an estimated weight of ~10 g per piece, consistent with typical Chinese peanut crisp confections. 9. Halal status is listed as false as no halal certification is stated in the source listing; this should be verified with the restaurant. 10. Vegan status is listed as false as some Chinese peanut crisp recipes include egg wash; this should be confirmed with the manufacturer.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full record from USDA FoodData Central adapted source; populated all multilingual names for Singaporean Chinese cuisine (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi); set GI as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; documented all -1 and 0.0 sentinel values in notes; verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list; confirmed all hard gates pass and score 8/8.

Iteration 2: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, as per GI classification rules. Updated corresponding text in health_context, llm_training.natural_language_description, and gi_source.source_match_notes. Corrected numerous per_100g values that were incorrectly set to -1.0 in the previous iteration, replacing them with actual numeric values from the cited USDA source (FDC ID 18166). Updated the 'notes' field to reflect these changes, removing justifications for values that are no longer -1.0. Re-verified all hard gates and criteria, resulting in a PASS decision.
